title: Die goldene Gans
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 64. Text from Wikisource („Die goldene Gans (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The sixty-fourth tale (KHM 64). A despised youngest son, called Dummling, shares his meagre meal with a little grey man and is rewarded with a golden goose whose feathers stick fast to all who grab at it, leading a comic train that at last makes a princess laugh. Older forms such as ward, gieng, thaten and heirathen appear.

sentences.t: Es war ein Mann, der hatte drei Söhne, davon hieß der jüngste der Dummling, und wurde verachtet und verspottet, und bei jeder Gelegenheit zurückgesetzt.
sentences.l: There was a man, who had three sons, of whom the youngest was called the Dummling, and was despised and mocked, and set back at every opportunity.
sentences.i: There was once a man who had three sons; the youngest of them was called the Simpleton, and he was despised and jeered at and slighted at every turn.
sentences.n: “Dummling” (from dumm, ‘stupid’) is the youngest son’s mocking nickname — the fairy-tale “Simpleton” who nonetheless wins out. It is a proper name here, so it is left untranslated in the reader. “davon hieß” = ‘of whom … was called.’

sentences.t: Es geschah, daß der älteste in den Wald gehen wollte, Holz hauen, und eh er gieng, gab ihm noch seine Mutter einen schönen feinen Eierkuchen und eine Flasche Wein mit, damit er nicht Hunger und Durst litte.
sentences.l: It happened that the eldest wanted to go into the forest, to chop wood, and ere he went, his mother gave him along a fine dainty pancake and a bottle of wine, so that he should suffer neither hunger nor thirst.
sentences.i: It came to pass that the eldest wished to go into the forest to chop wood, and before he set out his mother gave him a fine dainty pancake and a bottle of wine, so that he should not go hungry or thirsty.
sentences.n: “eh” is the archaic/colloquial form of ehe (‘before’). “litte” is subjunctive II of leiden (‘to suffer’) in a purpose clause — ‘so that he should not suffer.’ “mit … gab” is the separable geben … mit, ‘to give along.’

sentences.t: Als er in den Wald kam, begegnete ihm ein altes graues Männlein, das bot ihm einen guten Tag und sprach „gieb mir doch ein Stück Kuchen aus deiner Tasche, und laß mich einen Schluck von deinem Wein trinken, ich bin so hungrig und durstig.“
sentences.l: As he came into the forest, there met him an old grey little-man, who bade him a good day and spoke, “do give me a piece of cake out of your pocket, and let me drink a sip of your wine, I am so hungry and thirsty.”
sentences.i: When he came into the forest, an old grey little man met him, wished him good day, and said, “Do give me a piece of cake from your pocket and let me drink a sip of your wine; I am so hungry and thirsty.”
sentences.n: “begegnen” takes the dative (‘met him’). “einen guten Tag bieten” = to bid good day. “gieb” is the 1857 spelling of the imperative gib (‘give’).

sentences.t: Der kluge Sohn aber antwortete „geb ich dir meinen Kuchen und meinen Wein, so hab ich selber nichts, pack dich deiner Wege,“ ließ das Männlein stehen und gieng fort.
sentences.l: The clever son, however, answered, “if I give you my cake and my wine, then I have nothing myself, be off on your way,” left the little-man standing and went away.
sentences.i: But the clever son answered, “If I give you my cake and my wine, I’ll have nothing left myself — be off with you,” and he left the little man standing there and went on.
sentences.n: “geb ich dir” inverts to make a conditional without wenn: ‘if I give you.’ “pack dich deiner Wege” is a curt idiom, ‘be off, clear out’ (literally ‘pack yourself off on your ways’). “ließ … stehen” = left him standing.

sentences.t: Als er nun anfieng einen Baum zu behauen, dauerte es nicht lange, so hieb er fehl, und die Axt fuhr ihm in den Arm, daß er mußte heimgehen und sich verbinden lassen.
sentences.l: As he now began to hew at a tree, it lasted not long, then he struck amiss, and the axe drove into his arm, so that he had to go home and have himself bandaged.
sentences.i: Now, as he began to hew at a tree, it was not long before he struck amiss, and the axe went into his arm, so that he had to go home and have it bound up.
sentences.n: “hieb fehl” = struck amiss (fehl = ‘awry’). “daß er mußte heimgehen” — daß is the 1857 spelling of dass; note the finite verb mußte pulled forward before the infinitive, a common older word order.

sentences.t: Das war aber von dem grauen Männchen gekommen.
sentences.l: But that had come from the grey little-man.
sentences.i: But that had come about because of the grey little man.
sentences.n: “Männchen” (diminutive of Mann) alternates with Männlein through the tale — both mean ‘little man.’ The narrator quietly confirms the mishap was the old man’s doing.

sentences.t: Darauf gieng der zweite Sohn in den Wald, und die Mutter gab ihm, wie dem ältesten, einen Eierkuchen und eine Flasche Wein.
sentences.l: Thereupon the second son went into the forest, and the mother gave him, like the eldest, a pancake and a bottle of wine.
sentences.i: Then the second son went into the forest, and the mother gave him, as she had the eldest, a pancake and a bottle of wine.
sentences.n: “wie dem ältesten” = ‘as to the eldest,’ dative of comparison.

sentences.t: Dem begegnete gleichfalls das alte graue Männchen und hielt um ein Stückchen Kuchen und einen Trunk Wein an.
sentences.l: To him likewise the old grey little-man came, and applied for a little piece of cake and a draught of wine.
sentences.i: The old grey little man met him too, and asked for a little piece of cake and a draught of wine.
sentences.n: “hielt … an um” is the separable anhalten um, ‘to ask/apply for.’ “gleichfalls” (likewise) flags the repeating pattern of the folk tale — you will see it again.

sentences.t: Aber der zweite Sohn sprach auch ganz verständig „was ich dir gebe, das geht mir selber ab, pack dich deiner Wege,“ ließ das Männlein stehen und gieng fort.
sentences.l: But the second son too spoke quite sensibly, “what I give you, that I go without myself, be off on your way,” left the little-man standing and went away.
sentences.i: But the second son also answered very sensibly, “What I give you, I go without myself — be off with you,” and he left the little man standing and went on.
sentences.n: “das geht mir selber ab” — abgehen here = ‘to be lacking, to go without.’ The refusal echoes the eldest brother almost word for word, the tale’s rule of three.

sentences.t: Die Strafe blieb nicht aus, als er ein paar Hiebe am Baum gethan, hieb er sich ins Bein, daß er mußte nach Haus getragen werden.
sentences.l: The punishment did not fail to come; as he had done a couple of blows at the tree, he struck himself into the leg, so that he had to be carried home.
sentences.i: His punishment did not fail: when he had struck the tree a few blows, he cut himself in the leg, so that he had to be carried home.
sentences.n: “blieb nicht aus” = did not fail to come (ausbleiben). “gethan” is the 1857 spelling of getan (past participle of thun/tun).

sentences.t: Da sagte der Dummling „Vater, laß mich einmal hinaus gehen und Holz hauen.“
sentences.l: Then said the Dummling, “Father, let me for once go out and chop wood.”
sentences.i: Then the Simpleton said, “Father, let me go out this once and chop wood.”
sentences.n: “einmal” here softens the request, ‘just this once.’ “hinaus gehen” (to go out) is separable.

sentences.t: Antwortete der Vater „deine Brüder haben sich Schaden dabei gethan, laß dich davon, du verstehst nichts davon.“
sentences.l: Answered the father, “your brothers have done themselves harm at it, leave off from it, you understand nothing of it.”
sentences.i: The father answered, “Your brothers have come to harm at it; give it up, you know nothing about it.”
sentences.n: The tale often drops the subject-verb order and opens with the verb: “Antwortete der Vater” = ‘the father answered.’ “laß dich davon” = leave off, let it be. “davon” (of it) recurs three times — colloquial emphasis.

sentences.t: Der Dummling aber bat so lange, bis er endlich sagte „geh nur hin, durch Schaden wirst du klug werden.“
sentences.l: But the Dummling begged so long, until he finally said, “just go there, through harm you will grow clever.”
sentences.i: But the Simpleton begged so long that at last he said, “Go along, then; you’ll learn wisdom through harm.”
sentences.n: “durch Schaden klug werden” is a proverb, ‘to learn wisdom through harm’ — the English ‘once bitten, twice shy.’ Ironically the Simpleton is the one who needs no such lesson.

sentences.t: Die Mutter gab ihm einen Kuchen, der war mit Wasser in der Asche gebacken, und dazu eine Flasche saueres Bier.
sentences.l: The mother gave him a cake that was baked with water in the ashes, and besides a bottle of sour beer.
sentences.i: The mother gave him a cake baked with water in the ashes, and with it a bottle of sour beer.
sentences.n: His provisions are humbler than his brothers’ — plain ash-cake and sour beer instead of the fine pancake and wine. His generosity with so little is the point.

sentences.t: Als er in den Wald kam, begegnete ihm gleichfalls das alte graue Männchen, grüßte ihn und sprach „gieb mir ein Stück von deinem Kuchen und einen Trunk aus deiner Flasche, ich bin so hungrig und durstig.“
sentences.l: As he came into the forest, there met him likewise the old grey little-man, greeted him and spoke, “give me a piece of your cake and a draught out of your bottle, I am so hungry and thirsty.”
sentences.i: When he came into the forest, the old grey little man met him too, greeted him, and said, “Give me a piece of your cake and a draught from your bottle; I am so hungry and thirsty.”
sentences.n: The third meeting repeats the first two almost exactly — the pattern the listener now expects. What changes is the answer.

sentences.t: Antwortete der Dummling „ich habe aber nur Aschenkuchen und saueres Bier, wenn dir das recht ist, so wollen wir uns setzen und essen.“
sentences.l: Answered the Dummling, “I have but only ash-cake and sour beer, if that is right to you, then let us sit down and eat.”
sentences.i: The Simpleton answered, “But I have only ash-cake and sour beer; if that suits you, let us sit down and eat.”
sentences.n: “wenn dir das recht ist” = if that is agreeable to you (dative dir). Unlike his brothers, he offers to share what little he has.

sentences.t: Da setzten sie sich, und als der Dummling seinen Aschenkuchen herausholte, so wars ein feiner Eierkuchen, und das sauere Bier war ein guter Wein.
sentences.l: Then they sat down, and as the Dummling took out his ash-cake, it was a fine pancake, and the sour beer was a good wine.
sentences.i: So they sat down, and when the Simpleton drew out his ash-cake, it was a fine pancake, and the sour beer was a good wine.
sentences.n: “wars” = war es (‘it was’), a colloquial contraction. The humble fare is transformed — the reward for generosity.

sentences.t: Nun aßen und tranken sie, und danach sprach das Männlein „weil du ein gutes Herz hast und von dem Deinigen gerne mittheilst, so will ich dir Glück bescheren.
sentences.l: Now they ate and drank, and after that the little-man spoke, “because you have a good heart and gladly share of what is yours, so will I bestow fortune on you.
sentences.i: Now they ate and drank, and afterward the little man said, “Because you have a good heart and gladly share what is your own, I will grant you good fortune.
sentences.n: “von dem Deinigen” = ‘of what is yours,’ the substantivized possessive das Deinige. “Glück bescheren” = to bestow good fortune (bescheren is the verb for granting a gift, as at Christmas).

sentences.t: Dort steht ein alter Baum, den hau ab, so wirst du in den Wurzeln etwas finden.“
sentences.l: There stands an old tree, hew it down, then you will find something in the roots.”
sentences.i: There stands an old tree; cut it down, and you will find something among the roots.”
sentences.n: “den hau ab” = hew it down (abhauen, separable, imperative hau … ab). This single quotation runs across the sentence break — the little man’s speech continues from the previous unit.

sentences.t: Darauf nahm das Männlein Abschied.
sentences.l: Thereupon the little-man took leave.
sentences.i: With that the little man took his leave.
sentences.n: “Abschied nehmen” = to take one’s leave, say farewell (a fixed phrase).

sentences.t: Der Dummling gieng hin und hieb den Baum um, und wie er fiel, saß in den Wurzeln eine Gans, die hatte Federn von reinem Gold.
sentences.l: The Dummling went there and hewed the tree down, and as it fell, there sat in the roots a goose that had feathers of pure gold.
sentences.i: The Simpleton went and cut the tree down, and as it fell, there in the roots sat a goose with feathers of pure gold.
sentences.n: “hieb … um” = hewed down (umhauen, separable). “die hatte Federn” — the demonstrative die resumes the goose, a very common Grimm device for pointing back to a noun.

sentences.t: Er hob sie heraus, nahm sie mit sich und gieng in ein Wirthshaus, da wollte er übernachten.
sentences.l: He lifted it out, took it with him and went into an inn, where he wanted to stay the night.
sentences.i: He lifted it out, took it with him, and went into an inn, where he meant to spend the night.
sentences.n: “Wirthshaus” is the 1857 spelling of Wirtshaus (inn); the th- also appears in Wirth (innkeeper) and, throughout Grimm, in Thür, Thier and thun. “übernachten” = to stay overnight.

sentences.t: Der Wirth hatte aber drei Töchter, die sahen die Gans, waren neugierig was das für ein wunderlicher Vogel wäre und hätten gar gern eine von seinen goldenen Federn gehabt.
sentences.l: But the innkeeper had three daughters, who saw the goose, were curious what kind of a wondrous bird it might be, and would gladly have had one of its golden feathers.
sentences.i: Now the innkeeper had three daughters, who saw the goose, were curious what strange bird it might be, and would dearly have liked one of its golden feathers.
sentences.n: “was das für ein wunderlicher Vogel wäre” — ‘was für ein’ = ‘what kind of a,’ and wäre is subjunctive of reported curiosity. “hätten … gehabt” is subjunctive II, ‘would have had.’ Three daughters, the tale’s rule of three again.

sentences.t: Die älteste dachte „es wird sich schon eine Gelegenheit finden wo ich mir eine Feder ausziehen kann,“ und als der Dummling einmal hinaus gegangen war, faßte sie die Gans beim Flügel, aber Finger und Hand blieben ihr daran festhängen.
sentences.l: The eldest thought, “an opportunity will surely turn up where I can pull myself out a feather,” and when the Dummling had once gone out, she grasped the goose by the wing, but finger and hand stuck fast to it.
sentences.i: The eldest thought, “Some chance will surely come where I can pluck out a feather for myself,” and once the Simpleton had gone out, she seized the goose by the wing — but finger and hand stuck fast to it.
sentences.n: “schon” here isn’t ‘already’ but reassuring, ‘surely, sure enough.’ “festhängen” = to stick fast, hang fast (separable). The trap springs — everyone who touches the goose is caught.

sentences.t: Bald danach kam die zweite und hatte keinen andern Gedanken als sich eine goldene Feder zu holen: kaum aber hatte sie ihre Schwester angerührt, so blieb sie festhängen.
sentences.l: Soon after came the second and had no other thought than to fetch herself a golden feather: but scarcely had she touched her sister, then she stuck fast.
sentences.i: Soon after, the second came, with no other thought than to get herself a golden feather; but scarcely had she touched her sister than she too stuck fast.
sentences.n: “keinen andern Gedanken als … zu holen” = ‘no other thought than to fetch.’ “kaum … so” = ‘scarcely … when.’ The chain grows by one.

sentences.t: Endlich kam auch die dritte in gleicher Absicht: da schrieen die andern „bleib weg, ums Himmelswillen, bleib weg.“
sentences.l: At last the third also came in the same intention: then the others screamed, “stay away, for heaven’s sake, stay away.”
sentences.i: At last the third came too, with the same intent; then the others screamed, “Keep away, for heaven’s sake, keep away!”
sentences.n: “ums Himmelswillen” = for heaven’s sake (um … willen, ‘for the sake of,’ with contracted ums). “schrieen” is the 1857 spelling of schrien (screamed).

sentences.t: Aber sie begriff nicht warum sie wegbleiben sollte, dachte „sind die dabei, so kann ich auch dabei sein,“ und sprang herzu, und wie sie ihre Schwester angerührt hatte, so blieb sie an ihr hängen.
sentences.l: But she did not grasp why she should stay away, thought, “are they at it, then can I be at it too,” and sprang up, and as she had touched her sister, then she stuck to her.
sentences.i: But she could not understand why she should keep away; she thought, “If they’re in on it, so can I be,” and sprang up — and the moment she touched her sister, she stuck fast to her.
sentences.n: “begriff” = grasped, understood (begreifen). “dabei sein” = to be in on it, take part. Now all three sisters hang in a row.

sentences.t: So mußten sie die Nacht bei der Gans zubringen.
sentences.l: So they had to spend the night with the goose.
sentences.i: So they had to pass the night by the goose.
sentences.n: “zubringen” = to spend, pass (time), separable (die Nacht zubringen).

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen nahm der Dummling die Gans in den Arm, gieng fort, und bekümmerte sich nicht um die drei Mädchen, die daran hiengen.
sentences.l: On the next morning the Dummling took the goose in his arm, went away, and did not trouble himself about the three girls who hung on it.
sentences.i: The next morning the Simpleton took the goose under his arm, set off, and paid no heed to the three girls hanging onto it.
sentences.n: “am andern Morgen” = the next morning (andern = the old spelling of anderen). “sich bekümmern um” = to trouble oneself about, care about (reflexive). “hiengen” is the 1857 spelling of hingen (hung).

sentences.t: Sie mußten immer hinter ihm drein laufen, links und rechts, wies ihm in die Beine kam.
sentences.l: They had to run ever behind him, left and right, however it came into his legs.
sentences.i: They had to keep running after him, this way and that, wherever his legs happened to take him.
sentences.n: “wies ihm in die Beine kam” = wie es ihm … kam, ‘however it came into his legs,’ i.e. wherever his stride led. “drein laufen” = to run along behind (hinterdrein).

sentences.t: Mitten auf dem Felde begegnete ihnen der Pfarrer, und als er den Aufzug sah, sprach er „schämt euch, ihr garstigen Mädchen, was lauft ihr dem jungen Bursch durchs Feld nach, schickt sich das?“
sentences.l: In the middle of the field the parson met them, and as he saw the procession, he spoke, “shame yourselves, you nasty girls, why do you run after the young lad through the field, is that fitting?”
sentences.i: In the middle of the field the parson met them, and when he saw the procession he said, “Shame on you, you nasty girls! Why do you run after the young fellow across the field — is that seemly?”
sentences.n: “schämt euch” = shame on you (sich schämen, reflexive imperative). “schickt sich das?” = is that proper/seemly? (sich schicken = to be fitting). “Aufzug” = a procession, a spectacle.

sentences.t: Damit faßte er die jüngste an die Hand und wollte sie zurückziehen: wie er sie aber anrührte, blieb er gleichfalls hängen und mußte selber hinter drein laufen.
sentences.l: With that he grasped the youngest by the hand and wanted to pull her back: but as he touched her, he likewise stuck fast and had to run behind himself.
sentences.i: With that he seized the youngest by the hand and tried to draw her back; but as he touched her, he too stuck fast and had to run along behind them himself.
sentences.n: “zurückziehen” = to pull back (separable). The would-be rescuer is caught just like the girls — the running joke of the tale.

sentences.t: Nicht lange, so kam der Küster daher, und sah den Herrn Pfarrer, der drei Mädchen auf dem Fuß folgte.
sentences.l: Not long, then came the sexton along, and saw the lord parson, who followed three girls on foot’s heel.
sentences.i: Before long the sexton came by and saw the parson following three girls at his heels.
sentences.n: “Küster” = sexton, the parish clerk. “auf dem Fuß folgen” = to follow at one’s heels, close behind.

sentences.t: Da verwunderte er sich und rief „ei, Herr Pfarrer, wo hinaus so geschwind? vergeßt nicht daß wir heute noch eine Kindtaufe haben,“ lief auf ihn zu und faßte ihn am Ermel, blieb aber auch fest hängen.
sentences.l: Then he wondered and cried, “ei, lord parson, whither out so quickly? do not forget that we still have a christening today,” ran up to him and grasped him by the sleeve, but stuck fast too.
sentences.i: Then he marvelled and cried, “Why, master parson, where are you off to in such haste? Don’t forget we still have a christening today!” — and he ran up and grabbed him by the sleeve, but he too stuck fast.
sentences.n: “wo hinaus so geschwind?” = ‘where out so fast?’, elliptical for ‘where are you rushing off to?’. “vergeßt” is the 1857 spelling of vergesst (forget). “Ermel” is the 1857 spelling of Ärmel (sleeve). “Kindtaufe” = a christening.

sentences.t: Wie die fünf so hinter einander her trabten, kamen zwei Bauern mit ihren Hacken vom Feld: da rief der Pfarrer sie an und bat sie möchten ihn und den Küster los machen.
sentences.l: As the five so trotted one behind another, there came two peasants with their hoes from the field: then the parson called to them and begged they might make him and the sexton loose.
sentences.i: As the five went trotting along one behind another, two peasants came from the field with their hoes; the parson called out to them and begged them to free him and the sexton.
sentences.n: “hinter einander” = one behind another. “bat sie möchten … los machen” = begged that they might set free — möchten is subjunctive of reported request. “los machen” = to loosen, set free.

sentences.t: Kaum aber hatten sie den Küster angerührt, so blieben sie hängen, und waren ihrer nun siebene, die dem Dummling mit der Gans nachliefen.
sentences.l: But scarcely had they touched the sexton, then they stuck fast, and there were now seven of them who ran after the Dummling with the goose.
sentences.i: But scarcely had they touched the sexton than they stuck fast too, and now there were seven of them running after the Simpleton with the goose.
sentences.n: “waren ihrer nun siebene” = ‘there were now seven of them’ (ihrer = genitive ‘of them’; siebene is an older inflected form of sieben). The chain is complete at seven.

sentences.t: Er kam darauf in eine Stadt, da herrschte ein König, der hatte eine Tochter, die war so ernsthaft, daß sie niemand zum lachen bringen konnte.
sentences.l: He came thereupon into a city, where there ruled a king who had a daughter that was so serious that no one could bring her to laughter.
sentences.i: After that he came to a city where a king reigned who had a daughter so grave that no one could make her laugh.
sentences.n: “zum Lachen bringen” = to make (someone) laugh (bring to laughter). The sober princess who cannot laugh is a classic fairy-tale problem — and our procession is the cure.

sentences.t: Darum hatte er ein Gesetz gegeben, wer sie könnte zum lachen bringen, der sollte sie heirathen.
sentences.l: Therefore he had given a law: whoever could bring her to laughter, he should marry her.
sentences.i: So he had made a decree: whoever could make her laugh should marry her.
sentences.n: “ein Gesetz geben” = to issue a law/decree. “heirathen” is the 1857 spelling of heiraten (to marry).

sentences.t: Der Dummling, als er das hörte, gieng mit seiner Gans und ihrem Anhang vor die Königstochter, und als diese die sieben Menschen immer hinter einander herlaufen sah, fieng sie überlaut an zu lachen und wollte gar nicht wieder aufhören.
sentences.l: The Dummling, as he heard that, went with his goose and its train before the king’s daughter, and as she saw the seven people ever running one behind another, she began overloud to laugh and would not stop again at all.
sentences.i: When the Simpleton heard this, he went with his goose and its train before the king’s daughter; and when she saw the seven people all running one behind another, she burst out laughing aloud and would not stop at all.
sentences.n: “Anhang” = following, train — the string of people stuck to the goose. “fieng … an zu lachen” = began to laugh (anfangen, separable; fieng is the 1857 spelling of fing). “überlaut” = right out loud.

sentences.t: Da verlangte sie der Dummling zur Braut, aber dem König gefiel der Schwiegersohn nicht, er machte allerlei Einwendungen und sagte er müßte ihm erst einen Mann bringen, der einen Keller voll Wein austrinken könnte.
sentences.l: Then the Dummling demanded her for a bride, but to the king the son-in-law did not please; he made all sorts of objections and said he must first bring him a man who could drink out a cellar full of wine.
sentences.i: Then the Simpleton claimed her for his bride, but the king did not care for the son-in-law; he raised all manner of objections and said the Simpleton must first bring him a man who could drink up a whole cellar of wine.
sentences.n: “dem König gefiel … nicht” = ‘did not please the king’ (gefallen takes the dative). “müßte” is subjunctive of reported speech (‘he must,’ as the king said). “austrinken” = to drink up (separable). The king’s three impossible tasks now begin.

sentences.t: Der Dummling dachte an das graue Männchen, das könnte ihm wohl helfen, gieng hinaus in den Wald, und auf der Stelle, wo er den Baum abgehauen hatte, sah er einen Mann sitzen, der machte ein ganz betrübtes Gesicht.
sentences.l: The Dummling thought of the grey little-man, who might well help him, went out into the forest, and on the spot where he had hewn the tree off, he saw a man sitting who made a quite doleful face.
sentences.i: The Simpleton thought of the grey little man, who might well help him; he went out into the forest, and on the very spot where he had cut down the tree he saw a man sitting who wore a most doleful face.
sentences.n: “auf der Stelle” = on the spot. “abgehauen” = hewn off, cut down (abhauen, past participle). “ein betrübtes Gesicht machen” = to make/pull a sorrowful face.

sentences.t: Der Dummling fragte was er sich so sehr zu Herzen nähme.
sentences.l: The Dummling asked what he was taking so very much to heart.
sentences.i: The Simpleton asked what he was taking so much to heart.
sentences.n: “sich etwas zu Herzen nehmen” = to take something to heart. “nähme” is subjunctive II of nehmen in the indirect question.

sentences.t: Da antwortete er „ich habe so großen Durst, und kann ihn nicht löschen, das kalte Wasser vertrage ich nicht, ein Faß Wein habe ich zwar ausgeleert, aber was ist ein Tropfen auf einem heißen Stein?“
sentences.l: Then he answered, “I have such great thirst, and cannot quench it, the cold water I cannot tolerate, a cask of wine I have indeed emptied out, but what is a drop on a hot stone?”
sentences.i: Then he answered, “I am so terribly thirsty and cannot slake it; cold water I cannot abide. A cask of wine I have already emptied, true — but what is a drop on a hot stone?”
sentences.n: “löschen” = to quench (a thirst, a fire). “vertrage ich nicht” = I cannot tolerate/abide (vertragen). “Faß” is the 1857 spelling of Fass (cask). “ein Tropfen auf einem heißen Stein” = a drop on a hot stone, i.e. hopelessly too little (like our ‘a drop in the bucket’).

sentences.t: „Da kann ich dir helfen,“ sagte der Dummling, „komm nur mit mir, du sollst satt haben.“
sentences.l: “There I can help you,” said the Dummling, “just come with me, you shall have your fill.”
sentences.i: “There I can help you,” said the Simpleton; “just come along with me, and you shall drink your fill.”
sentences.n: “du sollst satt haben” = you shall have enough, be satisfied (satt = full, sated).

sentences.t: Er führte ihn darauf in des Königs Keller, und der Mann machte sich über die großen Fässer, trank und trank, daß ihm die Hüften weh thaten, und ehe ein Tag herum war, hatte er den ganzen Keller ausgetrunken.
sentences.l: He led him thereupon into the king’s cellar, and the man set upon the great casks, drank and drank, so that his hips ached, and before a day was round, he had drunk out the whole cellar.
sentences.i: Then he led him into the king’s cellar, and the man fell upon the great casks and drank and drank until his hips ached, and before a day was out he had drunk the whole cellar dry.
sentences.n: “sich über … machen” = to set upon, set to work on. “Fässer” is the 1857 plural of Faß. “thaten” is the 1857 spelling of taten (did); “weh thun” = to hurt, ache. “ausgetrunken” = drunk up, drained (austrinken).

sentences.t: Der Dummling verlangte abermals seine Braut, der König aber ärgerte sich daß ein schlechter Bursch, den jedermann einen Dummling nannte, seine Tochter davon tragen sollte, und machte neue Bedingungen: er müßte erst einen Mann schaffen, der einen Berg voll Brot aufessen könnte.
sentences.l: The Dummling demanded once again his bride, but the king was vexed that a poor lad, whom everyone called a Dummling, should carry off his daughter, and made new conditions: he must first procure a man who could eat up a mountain full of bread.
sentences.i: The Simpleton claimed his bride once more, but the king was annoyed that a lowly lad whom everyone called a Simpleton should carry off his daughter, and set new conditions: first he must find a man who could eat up a mountain of bread.
sentences.n: “ärgerte sich daß” = was annoyed that (sich ärgern). “Bursch” = lad, fellow. “davon tragen” = to carry off, bear away. “aufessen” = to eat up (separable). The second task.

sentences.t: Der Dummling besann sich nicht lange, sondern gieng gleich hinaus in den Wald: da saß auf demselben Platz ein Mann, der schnürte sich den Leib mit einem Riemen zusammen, machte ein grämliches Gesicht, und sagte „ich habe einen ganzen Backofen voll Raspelbrot gegessen, aber was hilft das, wenn man so großen Hunger hat, wie ich: mein Magen bleibt leer, und ich muß mich nur zuschnüren, wenn ich nicht Hungers sterben soll.“
sentences.l: The Dummling did not deliberate long, but rather went at once out into the forest: there sat on the same spot a man who laced his body together with a strap, made a peevish face, and said, “I have eaten a whole oven full of rasped bread, but what does that help, when one has such great hunger as I: my stomach stays empty, and I must just lace myself up, if I am not to die of hunger.”
sentences.i: The Simpleton did not ponder long, but went straight out into the forest; and there on the same spot sat a man cinching his body tight with a strap, wearing a sullen face, who said, “I have eaten a whole ovenful of rasped bread, but what good is that when a man is as hungry as I am? My stomach stays empty, and I must lace myself up tight, or I shall starve to death.”
sentences.n: “sich besinnen” = to reflect, deliberate. “Platz” = spot, place. “Riemen” = strap, belt. “Raspelbrot” = rasped bread, crusts ground and rebaked. “Hungers sterben” = to die of hunger (genitive of cause). “zuschnüren” = to lace/cinch up (separable).

sentences.t: Der Dummling war froh darüber, und sprach „mach dich auf und geh mit mir, du sollst dich satt essen.“
sentences.l: The Dummling was glad over that, and spoke, “make yourself up and go with me, you shall eat yourself full.”
sentences.i: The Simpleton was glad of that, and said, “Get yourself up and come with me, and you shall eat your fill.”
sentences.n: “mach dich auf” = get up, set off (sich aufmachen). “du sollst dich satt essen” = you shall eat your fill (sich satt essen).

sentences.t: Er führte ihn an den Hof des Königs, der hatte alles Mehl aus dem ganzen Reich zusammenfahren und einen ungeheuern Berg davon backen lassen: der Mann aber aus dem Walde stellte sich davor, fieng an zu essen, und in einem Tag war der ganze Berg verschwunden.
sentences.l: He led him to the court of the king, who had had all the flour from the whole realm carted together and an enormous mountain baked of it: but the man out of the forest placed himself before it, began to eat, and in one day the whole mountain was vanished.
sentences.i: He led him to the king’s court, where the king had had all the flour from the whole realm carted together and an enormous mountain of it baked; but the man from the forest planted himself before it, set to eating, and in a single day the whole mountain had vanished.
sentences.n: “zusammenfahren … lassen” = to have (something) carted together (causative lassen). “ungeheuern” = enormous, huge. “fieng an zu essen” = began to eat (anfangen). “verschwunden” = vanished (verschwinden).

sentences.t: Der Dummling forderte zum drittenmal seine Braut, der König aber suchte noch einmal Ausflucht, und verlangte ein Schiff das zu Land und zu Wasser fahren könnte: „so wie du aber damit angesegelt kommst,“ sagte er, „so sollst du gleich meine Tochter zur Gemahlin haben.“
sentences.l: The Dummling demanded for the third time his bride, but the king sought once again an evasion, and demanded a ship that could travel on land and on water: “but just as you come sailing up with it,” said he, “then you shall straightway have my daughter for a consort.”
sentences.i: The Simpleton claimed his bride a third time, but again the king sought a way out and demanded a ship that could sail on land and on water: “But the moment you come sailing up in it,” he said, “you shall have my daughter at once for your wife.”
sentences.n: “zum drittenmal” = for the third time — the last of the three tasks. “Ausflucht” = evasion, excuse. “angesegelt kommst” = come sailing up (ansegeln). “Gemahlin” = wife, consort (an elevated word).

sentences.t: Der Dummling gieng gerades Weges in den Wald, da saß das alte graue Männchen, dem er seinen Kuchen gegeben hatte, und sagte „ich habe für dich getrunken und gegessen, ich will dir auch das Schiff geben; das alles thu ich, weil du barmherzig gegen mich gewesen bist.“
sentences.l: The Dummling went straight-ways into the forest, there sat the old grey little-man, to whom he had given his cake, and said, “I have drunk and eaten for you, I will also give you the ship; all that I do because you have been merciful toward me.”
sentences.i: The Simpleton went straightway into the forest, and there sat the old grey little man to whom he had given his cake, who said, “I have drunk and eaten for you, and I will give you the ship as well; all this I do because you were merciful to me.”
sentences.n: “gerades Weges” = straightway, directly (genitive of manner; Weges from Weg). The little man reveals that the two hungry, thirsty men were himself all along. “barmherzig” = merciful, compassionate.

sentences.t: Da gab er ihm das Schiff, das zu Land und zu Wasser fuhr, und als der König das sah, konnte er ihm seine Tochter nicht länger vorenthalten.
sentences.l: Then he gave him the ship that travelled on land and on water, and as the king saw that, he could not withhold his daughter from him any longer.
sentences.i: Then he gave him the ship that went on land and on water, and when the king saw it, he could no longer keep his daughter from him.
sentences.n: “vorenthalten” = to withhold, keep back (from someone).

sentences.t: Die Hochzeit ward gefeiert, nach des Königs Tod erbte der Dummling das Reich, und lebte lange Zeit vergnügt mit seiner Gemahlin.
sentences.l: The wedding was celebrated, after the king’s death the Dummling inherited the realm, and lived a long time content with his consort.
sentences.i: The wedding was celebrated; after the king’s death the Simpleton inherited the kingdom, and lived long and happily with his wife.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘was’), used here as a passive auxiliary. “nach des Königs Tod” = after the king’s death (des Königs, genitive). The tale closes on the familiar note of the Simpleton rewarded.
